statement: Users do not scroll, so all important content must be placed above the fold.
why_it_spreads: Carried over from print and early-web eyetracking; designers fear content below the initial viewport will never be seen.
evidence_against: NN/g scrolling research (src-nngroup, https://www.nngroup.com/articles/scrolling-and-attention/): users do scroll, and more than they used to, when given a reason.
Responsive and mobile layouts make scrolling a primary, expected interaction.
replacement_principle: Assume users will scroll when motivated; use the top of the page to establish relevance and signal that more content follows, rather than cramming everything above the fold.
still_applies_when: Attention is still concentrated near the top, so lead with the most important content
Critical calls to action benefit from being visible without requiring a scroll on key pages
